document.critique: Rechecked capacities, all difference/same-machine rules, both exact-one constraints, and conditional implications. Candidate 1 survives every constraint and its costs sum to 166. Candidate 0 is feasible but costs 178; candidate 2 violates J3/J5 separation, candidate 4 violates J2’s machine restriction, and candidate 3’s stated total is arithmetically wrong (actual 193). Thus retain Candidate 1.

Rechecked all capacity and interaction rules; this assignment is feasible. I recalculated its cost as 29+10+9+9+8+13+24+10+15+4+13+32=176, so Candidate 4’s stated 157 fails. The other feasible candidates cost 170, 187, 193, and 202 after correction; thus this assignment remains optimal with corrected exact cost 176.

Tested all four candidates for machine counts, eligibility, pairwise rules, implications, and both exact-one constraints. All are feasible, but candidates 1–3 cost 164, 159, and 159; candidate 0 costs 155. Thus candidate 0 survives all checks and is the strongest answer, replacing the unavailable preceding proposal.

Tested all candidates against three-jobs-per-machine, pairwise, conditional, and exact-one constraints. Candidate 2 fails J1/J7 same-machine and J2/J3 different-machine rules. Candidates 0 and 1 survive, but cost 209 and 214. Candidate 3 survives every rule and sums exactly to 202, so it replaces the preceding proposal as the strongest answer.

I independently evaluated every candidate against the liar/truthful condition. Candidate 2 is the closest, but its H and O statements evaluate true and false respectively, conflicting with their assigned types; the other candidates have more decisive failures. Thus no listed candidate survives exactly, so this is not a justified unique solution without correcting the candidate set.

I re-evaluated every nested statement under the strongest candidate. Candidates 0, 1, 3, and 4 each fail directly at F, D, E, or multiple statements. Candidate 2 fails only because G is marked liar while G's statement evaluates true. Flipping G to truthful preserves all other evaluations, including D and E, so this revised assignment is the exact consistent answer.

Evaluated each nested statement under Candidate 1: all 17 statement truth values exactly match the assigned types. Candidate 0 fails at F, whose statement evaluates false despite F being marked truthful. Thus Candidate 1 survives the consistency test and should replace any preceding proposal.

I recomputed every nested statement under the strongest candidate variants. Candidates 0, 1, 3, and 4 fail directly on I or D; Candidate 2 fails only because D is labeled liar although D’s statement evaluates true. Flipping D to truthful preserves all other evaluations, yielding consistency for all 17 people, so this corrected assignment replaces the preceding proposal.

I rechecked the recurrence across the supplied terms and tested the candidate continuations against the stated modulus and coefficient constraints. Candidates 1–3 either repeat existing terms or introduce zeros/negative remainders and fail the reported remainder rules. Candidate 0 is the only viable nonnegative continuation and should replace the absent preceding proposal.

Rechecked the periodic-difference constraints across n=4, 8, and 12. Moduli 97 and 101 fail the resulting coefficient congruences; modulus 103 survives with t=2 and a consistent p[0],q[0] pair. Continuing the recurrence’s phase constraints selects the ordering [17,42,58], so Candidate 2 should replace the shifted Candidate 0.

The stated system is inconsistent, so no candidate is justified. Using n=4, 8, 12 and eliminating s and r gives 35(p0+q0)+23t ≡ 29 (mod M). Testing p0,q0,t ∈ {-2,-1,1,2} and M ∈ {97,101,103} yields no solution. Thus Candidate 1 cannot be verified; the returned array is only a placeholder, and the problem data need correction.

I rechecked the recurrence indexing, periodic coefficient selection, modular remainder range, and the n=4–17 constraints against all three allowed moduli and coefficient domains. The zero sequence fails immediately, and the alternatives do not satisfy the full constraint set. The surviving unique continuation is [18, 94, 17].
